strands.title: Replay Module Requirements & Design Intent
strands.content: The replay module (src/pichay/replay.py) must reconstruct offline simulations of context paging behavior from proxy JSONL logs. Core purpose: enable post-hoc evaluation of paging effectiveness by replaying exact message array states at each API call turn, applying compaction logic to detect page faults (model re-requesting evicted content), and computing aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). This bridges proxy logs to pager compaction logic. User mandate: 'Read the existing pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py thoroughly before writing any code. Match the coding style and patterns.' The module must have a CLI entry point with argparse supporting --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags. Page faults occur when model's next action re-requests content that was evicted by prior paging operations. The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics. The project is named 'Pichay' (Quechua: 'to sweep, to clean'), explicitly designed to measure the effect of removing dead tool outputs from agentic LLM context windows.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs to reconstruct paging scenarios
The module must reconstruct the messages array as it would have existed at each API call turn
Page faults occur when the model's actual next action re-requests content that was evicted
The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction %)
A CLI entry point with argparse is required with --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags
Pichay is a Quechua term meaning 'to sweep, to clean', capturing the project's purpose of removing dead tool outputs

strands.title: Proxy Log Schema — PARTIALLY RESOLVED via Direct Truncation Recovery
strands.content: After four failed full-file reads, cycle 4 achieved partial schema breakthrough via tool_result from a read operation (turn 17). The result returned only the opening of a JSONL record but crucially revealed the actual structure: {"type": "request", "timestamp": "ISO-8601 format", "model": "model name", "max_tokens": integer, "stream": boolean, "system": {"system_prompt_bytes": integer, "system_prompt_typ": [incomplete, but field name visible]}. This confirms: (1) Records are JSON objects with type field ('request'), (2) Timestamps use ISO-8601 format with microsecond precision and timezone, (3) Request metadata includes model, max_tokens, stream flag, and nested system dict containing prompt metrics. The record was truncated at 'system_prompt_typ[' suggesting either 'system_prompt_type' or similar. This is PARTIAL RESOLUTION: the schema has been glimpsed but not fully captured. The bottleneck remains unbroken—we have confirmation that JSONL records exist with this structure, but the full record depth (whether messages array is included, what other fields exist, what the full system dict contains) is still unknown. The truncation is now known to occur mid-field, not at line boundaries, suggesting output buffering or token limit interaction.

key_claims.text: Proxy JSONL records contain at minimum: type, timestamp (ISO-8601), model, max_tokens, stream, system dict
The system dict contains system_prompt_bytes and a field starting with 'system_prompt_typ' (likely 'system_prompt_type')
Records are truncated mid-field, suggesting token limit or output buffer constraint rather than line-based truncation
The full depth of JSONL records (including whether messages array is logged) remains unknown

strands.title: Critical Implementation Unknowns — Partially Resolved, Core Gaps Persist
strands.content: The JSONL schema is now partially known (basic structure confirmed), but essential gaps remain: (1) Whether the full messages array is stored in proxy logs — critical unknown, as this is the core requirement for replay. The assistant's turn 18 comment explicitly states 'the proxy log stores metrics about messages but not the full messages array' and concludes 'For replay to work, I need to also log the full messages.' This indicates the assistant's inference that the current proxy logging does NOT include full message content, which would require a design change to the proxy itself. (2) pager.py API — still unknown (no source code retrieved). (3) Exact page fault detection method — still unknown. (4) Metrics calculation — still unknown. (5) eval.py patterns — still unknown. (6) Whether existing tests exist for replay or proxy behavior — a glob search for test files (turn 19) returned 'No files found', suggesting either no tests exist in the expected location or the glob pattern was incorrect. The turn 18 comment represents a CRITICAL INFERENCE SHIFT: the assistant has reasoned that the proxy logs are insufficient for replay as currently designed, and that the proxy itself needs modification to log the messages array. This is a design constraint, not merely an information gap.

key_claims.text: The current proxy logs do not include the full messages array — only metrics about messages
To implement replay, the proxy logging itself must be modified to capture full message content
pager.py API (function signatures, invocation patterns, return types) has not been retrieved
No test files were found in expected locations (glob returned empty result in turn 19)
The operational definition of page fault detection (how to identify re-requested evicted content) remains unclear

strands.title: Inferential Reasoning About Proxy Requirements — Assistant's Analytical Leap
strands.content: In turn 18, the assistant moved from attempting to extract information to making explicit inference: 'I see that the proxy log stores metrics about messages but not the full messages array. For replay to work, I need to also log the full messages.' This inference is based on comparing the revealed JSONL schema (which shows only metadata fields, not a messages array field) against the replay module's stated requirement to 'reconstruct exact message array states at each API call turn.' The reasoning is sound: page fault detection requires knowing what the model is requesting (which requires the messages array) and what was evicted (which requires state tracking). The assistant's conclusion that proxy.py must be modified to log messages is a logical inference from incomplete information, but it introduces a NEW REQUIREMENT not explicitly stated by the user. The user did not say 'the proxy logs don't include messages' — the assistant inferred this. This inference may be correct, but it also may be premature. The schema may simply have been truncated before reaching a messages field. The turn 18 comment should be treated as a hypothesis, not a resolved fact. The subsequent glob search for test files (turn 19) may have been an attempt to find evidence supporting this hypothesis (test cases that demonstrate what data the proxy logs), but the empty result leaves the hypothesis unvalidated.

key_claims.text: The assistant inferred that proxy logs lack messages array by noting it was absent from the partially-revealed schema
This inference is logically sound but may be premature — the schema may have been truncated before messages field
The assistant's conclusion that proxy.py must be modified is a hypothesis, not a validated fact
The glob search for tests was likely an attempt to validate the proxy data format hypothesis

strands.title: Next Cycle Urgency — Validation Required Before Architecture Decision
strands.content: The key branching point is now whether the proxy logs include the full messages array. This question must be resolved before proceeding, because it determines the entire architecture: (1) If messages ARE in the logs → replay.py can be implemented as originally scoped (parse logs, apply pager, detect faults, compute stats). (2) If messages are NOT in the logs → replay.py cannot be implemented without modifying proxy.py to log them, which is scope creep and requires user approval. The turn 18 inference that they are not included is based on a truncated schema view and should be validated. The next cycle must either: (a) Retrieve the full JSONL schema to see if messages field exists, (b) Ask the user directly: 'Does the proxy log include the full messages array?', (c) Examine proxy.py code to see what it logs (read attempt has failed 4 times; may fail again), or (d) Ask the user to clarify the replay design: 'Should replay reconstruct messages from the logs, or from some other source?'. The glob search returning empty suggests the assistant was looking for test evidence but found none. This is actually a critical signal: if no tests exist for proxy logging behavior, the internal data format may be undocumented, requiring direct user input.

key_claims.text: Whether proxy logs include the full messages array is the critical unresolved question
This question determines whether replay.py is in-scope or requires proxy.py modification
The assistant's inference that they are not included is based on incomplete schema and requires validation
Empty glob result for tests suggests proxy logging may be undocumented, requiring direct user input

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Complete JSONL record structure from the schema breakthrough (turn 17)
declared_losses.why: The tool_result in turn 17 displayed a single JSON record opening but was truncated mid-field (at 'system_prompt_typ['). Only the first few fields were visible. The truncation prevents seeing whether a messages array exists later in the record, what other top-level fields exist, or the full structure of nested dicts. This is a context_pressure constraint—the output buffer or token limit caused mid-field truncation rather than line-based or record-based truncation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Validation of the turn 18 inference that proxy logs lack messages array
declared_losses.why: The assistant inferred this from the absence of a messages field in the truncated schema, but the truncation makes this inference epistemically weak. If the schema had been complete, confirmation would be immediate. The subsequent glob search (turn 19) likely intended to validate this via test files, but returned empty. Without validation, the inference remains a hypothesis. Declared as authorial_choice because the assistant proceeded with reasoning despite incomplete information.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Confidence in continuing with source code inspection as the primary information retrieval strategy
declared_losses.why: After four failed read attempts and one successful truncated glimpse, the pattern is clear: file reading returns truncated results. The turn 18 comment and turn 19 glob search represent a shift away from code inspection toward inference and hypothesis testing. This is a practical_constraint—the read tool is unreliable for full file content, forcing a pivot to direct user questioning or different retrieval methods.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Test evidence for proxy data format and replay behavior
declared_losses.why: The glob search for test files returned 'No files found' (turn 19). This eliminates a potential evidence source. Whether this means tests don't exist, are located elsewhere, or the glob pattern was wrong is unknown. The loss is the ability to infer data format and expectations from test cases. Declared as practical_constraint because the tool search itself worked, but returned null results.

open_questions: Does the proxy JSONL log include the full messages array for each API call, or only metadata?
If messages are not logged, should replay.py be modified to reconstruct them from some other source, or does proxy.py need to be extended to log them?
What is the complete schema of a single JSONL record in the proxy log (all fields, including nested structures)?
What does the truncated 'system_prompt_typ' field represent, and what are the downstream fields after it?
Why did the glob search for test files return empty — are there no tests, or are they in an unexpected location?
What is the pager.py API (function signatures, how compaction is invoked, what data structures it modifies)?
How is 'page fault' operationally defined and detected (what condition triggers it)?
What are the exact definitions and calculation methods for 'fault rate', 'bytes saved', and 'reduction percentage'?
